does: This paper introduces RealDPO, a preference learning framework that applies Direct Preference Optimization to video generation by using real-world video data as positive examples contrasted against model-generated errors. The method employs a specialized loss function that treats realistic human motions from actual videos as preferred samples and compares them against erroneous outputs from the generative model. The authors develop RealAction-5K, a curated dataset of 5,000 high-quality videos depicting diverse human daily activities with detailed motion information, to support preference learning. Through iterative self-correction enabled by this real-vs-generated comparison, the approach progressively improves motion synthesis quality beyond what traditional supervised fine-tuning achieves, addressing the persistent challenge of generating natural, smooth, and contextually consistent complex human motions in video generation.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in video generative models and extends the emerging paradigm of using preference optimization for model alignment. It directly responds to limitations of supervised fine-tuning in capturing motion realism and connects to the broader adoption of Direct Preference Optimization techniques pioneered in language models and increasingly applied to multimodal domains. The paper leverages insights from human preference learning and contrastive training methodologies, adapting them specifically to the domain of motion synthesis. It also engages with the growing recognition that real-world data provides stronger training signals than synthetic annotations, a principle successfully applied in other generative tasks. By framing motion quality as a preference problem rather than pure supervised learning, it connects to recent work on alignment and post-training refinement in generative models.
enables: RealDPO establishes a new pipeline for refining video generation models through preference-based learning, potentially inspiring similar approaches in other motion-heavy generative tasks like dance synthesis, sports motion generation, or robotic movement simulation. The method enables more fine-grained control over specific motion attributes by leveraging natural distributions in real video, allowing future work to incorporate additional preference signals beyond realism. The introduced RealAction-5K dataset provides infrastructure for advancing motion synthesis research more broadly. This alignment approach opens pathways toward video models that better capture complex human dynamics, which could improve applications in content creation, animation, virtual reality, and human-computer interaction. The framework also suggests generalizable principles for applying preference optimization to multimodal generative models where real-world reference data is abundant.
